question: In the ‘Render to Caesar’ controversy, what is the hidden trap in Genesis 1 (‘image’) that Jesus leverages—and how does that undercut simplistic ‘Bible endorses theocracy’ claims?

choices.A: Caesar gets the coin bearing his image, but God is owed the human person bearing God’s image—distinguishing political duty from ultimate allegiance.
choices.B: It teaches money is evil and should be rejected.
choices.C: It teaches the state is divine and should be worshiped.
choices.D: It proves the Bible forbids paying all taxes.

teaching.title: Image-language: coin vs human
teaching.body: Jesus’ reply turns on ‘image’: the coin bears Caesar’s image, but humans bear God’s image. That implies limited political obligation and ultimate divine ownership of the person. It’s a tidy rebuttal to claims that the text is simply pushing an uncritical theocracy.
